draft.introduction: Firdowsi’s Shahnameh (c. 1010 CE) is the national epic of Iran, recounting the mythical and historical past of Persia in approximately 50,000 couplets. It preserved the Persian language and cultural identity during the centuries of Arab political dominance.

translation.text: In the name of the Lord of soul and wisdom, beyond whom thought cannot rise. The Lord of name and the Lord of place, the Lord of sustenance and the Lord of guidance. The Lord of the sky and the revolving sphere, who illumines the moon, Venus, and the sun. He is above all name, all thought, all place. He is the creator, and creation is his sign.
translation.original_text: به نام خداوند جان و خرد / کزین برتر اندیشه برنگذرد

translator_notes.note: Firdowsi’s invocation declares God to be beyond all thought and naming, a position consistent with both Islamic theology and the Neoplatonic apophatic tradition that was well known in Persian intellectual circles. The Shahnameh is written entirely in Persian, deliberately avoiding Arabic loanwords—a linguistic act of cultural preservation.
